title: Why being vulnerable in front of others feels like dying
author: Kalesh

metaDescription:
Your body thinks you're about to die

htmlContent:
Your body thinks you're about to die

I remember sitting across from someone I trusted. Someone safe. And I needed to tell them something real. Something I'd never said out loud. My throat closed up. My hands went cold. My heart started slamming against my ribs like it was trying to escape. And a voice in my head screamed: Stop. Do not say it. You will die.

I didn't say it. I smiled, changed the subject, and walked away feeling like I'd just survived something. But I hadn't survived anything. I'd just confirmed to my nervous system that vulnerability is dangerous. That silence keeps me alive. That connection isn't worth the risk.

Here's the thing. That feeling of dying when you're about to be vulnerable? It's not dramatic. It's not you being weak or broken. It's your nervous system doing exactly what it learned to do to keep you alive. And until you understand that, you'll keep running from the one thing that might actually help you heal.

Stephen Porges, the neuroscientist who developed Polyvagal Theory, explains this better than anyone. He showed that our nervous system is constantly scanning for safety and danger. This happens below conscious awareness. Your body is reading micro-expressions, tone of voice, posture, energy. It's asking: Is it safe to connect? Or do I need to shut down?

When you grew up in an environment where vulnerability was punished, where showing your real feelings got you hurt, ignored, or shamed, your nervous system learned one thing loud and clear: Being seen is dangerous. And now, even in safe situations with safe people, your body still sounds the alarm. Because your nervous system doesn't care about the present moment. It cares about survival. And it learned that the best way to survive is to stay hidden.

So when you feel like you're dying before you open your mouth to tell someone the truth? That's not weakness. That's your ventral vagal nerve going offline. That's your body switching into dorsal vagal shutdown. That's the oldest part of your brain saying: Play dead. Disappear. Make yourself small. It's the only way.

The lie of "just be vulnerable"

The wellness industry loves to tell you to be vulnerable. Just open up. Just share your truth. Just let people in. As if vulnerability is a choice you can make with your conscious mind. As if your nervous system will just fall in line because you read a quote on Instagram.

That's not how it works. And honestly, that advice can do real damage.

I've seen people push themselves to be vulnerable before their bodies were ready. They force themselves to share in support groups. They confess their deepest shame to partners who haven't earned their trust. They open up in therapy before they have any regulation skills. And then they crash. They dissociate. They feel worse than before. And they conclude: See? Vulnerability is dangerous. I was right all along.

Bessel van der Kolk, who wrote The Body Keeps the Score, spent decades studying how trauma lives in the body. He found that trauma isn't just a memory stored in your brain. It's a physical imprint. Your body holds the experience. Your nervous system keeps the score. And no amount of talking about your feelings will change that if your body still believes it's in danger.

A book that many trauma survivors find genuinely helpful is The Body Keeps the Score (paid link). It explains why your body reacts the way it does. Why you can't just think your way out of this. Why vulnerability feels like death. Because your body remembers what your mind has tried to forget.

So when someone tells you to just be vulnerable, they're asking you to override millions of years of evolutionary programming. They're asking you to ignore what your body knows. That's not healing. That's re-enactment. That's asking a burn victim to touch a hot stove and call it courage.

Real vulnerability isn't something you force. It's something your nervous system allows when it finally feels safe enough. And building that safety takes time. It takes skills. It takes understanding what's actually happening inside you.

The dorsal vagal trap

Let me get specific about what's happening in your body when vulnerability feels like dying. Stay with me here, because this is the part that actually helps.

Your autonomic nervous system has three main states. The ventral vagal state is social engagement. It's connection, safety, calm. This is where vulnerability is possible. The sympathetic state is fight or flight. It's activation, mobilization, energy. And the dorsal vagal state is shutdown. It's collapse, dissociation, freezing. This is where you feel numb, disconnected, like you're disappearing.

When you try to be vulnerable and your body screams no, you're likely dropping from ventral or sympathetic straight into dorsal. You're going offline. Your body is playing dead. And that feeling of dying? It's not metaphorical. Dorsal vagal shutdown mimics death. Your heart rate drops. Your breathing slows. Your awareness narrows. You feel like you're floating away or sinking into the ground.

Your body is literally activating the same neural pathways that would be active if you were hemorrhaging or freezing to death. Because that's what dorsal vagal is for. It's the oldest, most primitive survival response. It's the system that says: If you can't fight and you can't flee, then maybe if you look dead enough, the predator will lose interest.

Only the predator isn't a tiger. It's a partner asking how you feel. It's a friend wanting to know what's really going on. It's a therapist waiting for you to speak. Your body doesn't know the difference. It just knows: Danger. Shut down. Survive.

One supplement that many people find calming for their nervous system is Doctors Best Magnesium Glycinate (paid link). Magnesium glycinate is easily absorbed and can help quiet the nervous system without making you feel drugged. It's not a cure, but it can help take the edge off when your body is constantly on alert.

I want you to notice something. When you read that, did your body relax even a little? Or did it tighten up because I suggested something that might help? Notice what just happened in your body. That's the information you need to pay attention to. Your body is always telling you where it is. You just have to learn to listen.

Building the capacity for connection

So what do you actually do about this? How do you get from feeling like you're dying every time someone sees you to actually being able to connect?

The answer isn't what you think. It's not about forcing vulnerability. It's not about pushing through the fear. It's not about positive affirmations or ation or any of that noise.

It's about capacity. You have to build your nervous system's capacity to tolerate connection without going into survival mode. This is a physical process. It happens in your body, not in your thoughts.

Here's what that looks like in practice. You start small. Not with telling someone your deepest trauma. But with making eye contact for one extra second. With saying "I'm not okay" when someone asks how you are, without explaining further. With letting someone see your face before you put on the mask.

And when your body starts to panic, you pause. You don't push through. You stop. You put a hand on your chest. You take a slow breath. You orient to the room. You notice that you're not actually dying. You're just feeling the old fear. And you let it pass through you without acting on it.

Janina Fisher, who wrote about trauma-informed stabilization, talks about this as "pendulation." You move between connection and disconnection. Between vulnerability and safety. You learn to touch the edge of your window of tolerance without falling out of it. And over time, that window widens.

A structured workbook that many C-PTSD survivors find practical is The Complex PTSD Workbook (paid link). It has exercises that help you track your nervous system states and build regulation skills gradually. Not by forcing vulnerability, but by understanding your own patterns.

You also need to learn to discern. Not everyone deserves your vulnerability. Not every situation is safe. Part of healing is learning to trust your discernment again. To know when it's actually dangerous and when it just feels dangerous. That's a skill. And you develop it by testing the waters, not by jumping in the deep end.

Some people will never be safe for you to be vulnerable with. And that's not a failure. That's wisdom. Your nervous system was right about some people. The work is learning to tell the difference between the people who hurt you and the people who won't.

A discreet option for reducing noise sensitivity is Loop Quiet 2 Ear Plugs (paid link). They lower noise without blocking it completely, which can help your nervous system feel safer in overwhelming environments. Sometimes the first step toward connection is reducing sensory overload.

What happens when you stop running

I'm not going to tell you that if you do all this, vulnerability will feel easy. It might never feel easy. It might always feel like something that costs you something. But it might stop feeling like death.

And that's the goal. Not to be fearless. But to be able to feel the fear and still choose connection. To have your body scream at you to run and to say: I hear you. I know you're trying to protect me. But I've got this. We're safe now.

That conversation with your nervous system takes time. It takes repetition. It takes failing and trying again. It takes letting yourself be seen in small ways and surviving it. It takes proving to your body, over and over, that being known doesn't mean being destroyed.

I still feel the pull to disappear when someone sees me too clearly. I still feel my throat close up when I'm about to say something real. But I've learned to breathe into it. To stay in my body. To remind myself that I'm an adult now, and I can handle whatever comes.

And sometimes I still run. Sometimes I still hide. Sometimes I still smile and change the subject. That's not failure. That's being human. That's having a nervous system that learned to survive in a world that wasn't safe. And the fact that I notice it now, that I can choose differently sometimes, that's the healing.

You don't have to be perfectly vulnerable. You don't have to share everything. You don't have to let everyone in. You just have to start building the capacity to let someone in, somewhere, sometimes. And let that be enough.

Your body learned to protect you. It did its job. And now you get to teach it that it doesn't have to work so hard anymore. That you're here. That you've got this. That being seen might not kill you after all.

It might even save you.
